title: Apply: The reasons for the Revolution
description: Apply your understanding of the reasons for the American Revolution in this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Principles of the American Revolution: The Ideas and Events That Led to Change
Apply: The reasons for the Revolution

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from *Common Sense* (1776) by Thomas Paine.**

To the evil of monarchy we have added that of hereditary succession; and as the first is a [[☃ definition 1]] and lessening of ourselves, so the second, claimed as a matter of right, is an insult and imposition on [[☃ definition 2]]. For all men being originally equals, no one by birth could have a right to set up his own family in perpetual preference to all others for ever, and tho' himself might deserve some decent degree of honours of his contemporaries, yet his descendants might be far too unworthy to inherit them.



**This excerpt contributed most directly to which development?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a decline to low, poor, and unvalued state

choices.content: The colonists’ belief in the superiority of republican forms of government
The colonists’ belief that there should be no government
Increased support for property qualifications for voting, restricting suffrage to elite men
Increased support for replacing of the Articles of Confederation with a strong central government

assessment_items.question_content:
**This excerpt is from *Common Sense* (1776) by Thomas Paine.**

To the evil of monarchy we have added that of hereditary succession; and as the first is a [[☃ definition 1]] and lessening of ourselves, so the second, claimed as a matter of right, is an insult and imposition on [[☃ definition 2]]. For all men being originally equals, no one by birth could have a right to set up his own family in perpetual preference to all others for ever, and tho' himself might deserve some decent degree of honours of his contemporaries, yet his descendants might be far too unworthy to inherit them.

**Which development best represents a logical extension of the ideas expressed in the excerpt?**



[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a decline to low, poor, and unvalued state

choices.content: The expansion of voting rights to all men and women
The creation of a republican form of government
Ending the practice of passing property on to heirs
Increasing support for westward expansion

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a *Boston Gazette* article (1765) by Francis Bernard.**

The people of *North America*, at this time, expect a [[☃ definition 1]] and reformation of the *American* Governments, and are better disposed to submit to it than ever they were, or perhaps ever will be again.

This is therefore the proper and critical time to reform the *American* governments upon a general, constitutional, firm, and durable plan; and if it is not done now, it will probably every day grow more difficult, till at last it becomes [[☃ definition 2]].

**What argument does Bernard make in the passage?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The American colonial governments are making alliances with the French.
The American colonies want representation in Parliament.
The American colonial governments need to be reformed.
The American colonies want to rebel against the British crown.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from a *Boston Gazette* article (1765) by Francis Bernard.**

The people of *North America*, at this time, expect a [[☃ definition 1]] and reformation of the *American* Governments, and are better disposed to submit to it than ever they were, or perhaps ever will be again.

This is therefore the proper and critical time to reform the *American* governments upon a general, constitutional, firm, and durable plan; and if it is not done now, it will probably every day grow more difficult, till at last it becomes [[☃ definition 2]].

**The developments that Bernard describes led to which event?**



[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The formal declaration of independence from the British
The formation of the first colonial congress
The westward expansion of American colonies
The removal of British military from the colonies

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *A Report of the Record Commissioners of the City of Boston, Containing the Boston Town Records, 1758 to 1769.***

But what still heightens our [[☃ definition 1]] is that those unexpected proceedings may be preparatory to new Taxations upon us: For if our Trade may be taxed why not our Lands? Why not the produce of our Lands and every Thing we possess or make use of? This we [[☃ definition 2]] annihilates our Charter Right to Govern and Tax ourselves.—It strikes at our British Privileges which as we have never forfeited them we hold in common with our Fellow Subjects who are Natives of Britain: If Taxes are laid upon us in any shape without ever having a legal representation where they are laid, are we not reduced from the Character of Free Subjects to the miserable state of [[☃ definition 3]] Slaves?

**What concern does the author raise in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: under the control of another and having to pay for protection

choices.content: The British government wanted to abolish slavery.
The colonies could not trade with other countries.
The colonies had no formal governments.
The British government began taxing the colonies.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *A Report of the Record Commissioners of the City of Boston, Containing the Boston Town Records, 1758 to 1769.***

But what still heightens our [[☃ definition 1]] is that those unexpected proceedings may be preparatory to new Taxations upon us: For if our Trade may be taxed why not our Lands? Why not the produce of our Lands and every Thing we possess or make use of? This we [[☃ definition 2]] annihilates our Charter Right to Govern and Tax ourselves.—It strikes at our British Privileges which as we have never forfeited them we hold in common with our Fellow Subjects who are Natives of Britain: If Taxes are laid upon us in any shape without ever having a legal representation where they are laid, are we not reduced from the Character of Free Subjects to the miserable state of [[☃ definition 3]] Slaves?

**This excerpt best supports which argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: under the control of another and having to pay for protection

choices.content: Colonists believed themselves to be exempt from any British laws because they considered themselves Americans first.
Colonists saw themselves as loyal British citizens who had to follow any laws passed by Parliament, including tax laws.
Colonists saw themselves as British citizens and considered new taxes without representation in Parliament as a violation of their rights.
Colonists believed themselves to be colonial citizens who were governed only by the laws passed by the colonial congress.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the postscript to the *Pennsylvania Gazette* (1773).**

 We are positively informed, that the patriotic inhabitants of Lexington, at a [[☃ definition 1]] meeting, unanimously resolved against the use of Bohea tea of all sorts, Dutch or English importation and to [[☃ definition 2]] the sincerity of their resolution, they brought together every ounce contained in the town, and committed it to one common bonfire. 

We are also informed, Charlestown is in motion to follow their [[☃ definition 3]] example.

*Query.* Would it not materially affect the bringing this detestable herb into disuse, if every town would enjoin their Select-men to deny licenses to all houses of entertainment, who were known to afford tea to their guests?

 Our reason for suggesting this is, the difficulty these people are under to avoid dishing out this poison, without such a provision in their favor.

**What caused the protest described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The start of the Revolutionary War against the British
The punishment of participants in the Boston Tea Party
The removal of British troops protecting the borders
Taxation on imported goods

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the postscript to the *Pennsylvania Gazette* (1773).**

 Yesterday we had a greater Meeting of the Body than ever, the Country coming in from 20 Miles round, and every Step was taken that was practicable for returning the Teas. The Moment it was known out of the Doors, that Mr. Rotch could not obtain a Pass for his Ship by the Castle, a Number of People [[☃ definition 1]] in the Street, and in a very little Time, every Ounce of the Teas on board of Captains Hall, Bruce, and Coffin, was immersed in the Bay, without the least Injury to private Property.

**What was the British response to this act of protest?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: A series of acts that aimed to force the colonies to follow British laws
The  removal of all taxes on the American colonies
The appointment of a colonial representative in British Parliament
A declaration of war against Massachusetts and other American colonies

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from Richard Bland’s “An Enquiry into the Rights of the British Colonies,” which was published in *The Political Register and Impartial Review of New Books for 1769*.**

I have undertaken to examine, with an honest plainness and freedom, whether the [[☃ definition 1]], by imposing taxes upon the colonies by authority of parliament, have pursued a wise and [[☃ definition 2]] plan of government, or whether they have exerted [[☃ definition 3]] and destructive acts of power. . . . 

I cannot comprehend, how men, who are excluded from voting at the election of members of parliament, can be represented in that assembly; or how those, who are elected, do not sit in the house as representatives of their [[☃ definition 4]]. These assertions appear to me not only [[☃ definition 5]], but contrary to the fundamental principles of the *English* constitution. 

**Which statement best summarizes Bland’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: government under the leadership of a prime minister
the people whom an elected official represents

choices.content: Any measures passed by the English Parliament are invalid, as the colonists’ representatives voted against taxation by the British.
The taxes that the colonists are required to pay are lawful and appropriate based on Parliament’s authority under the English constitution.
Any taxes levied on the colonies are invalid, as the colonists do not have representation in Parliament.
The colonies are facing a just punishment that limits their voting power because they refuse to pay their taxes.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from James Wilson’s “On the Legislative Authority of the British Parliament” (1774).**

[T]he principles on which we have founded our opposition to the [[☃ definition 1]] acts of parliament, are the principles of justice and freedom, and of the British constitution. . . . All men are, by nature, equal and free: no one has a right to any authority over another without his consent: all lawful government is founded on the consent of those who are subject to it: such consent was given with a view to ensure and to increase the happiness of the governed, above what they could enjoy in an independent state of nature. The consequence is, that the happiness of the society is the *first* law of every government. . . . 

**Which statement best summarizes Wilson’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The colonists’ opposition to recent acts of Parliament is based on the fundamental idea that all people are free and inherently equal.
The colonists’ protest of the recent British acts contradicts the principles of the British constitution, which gives parliament supreme authority.
The colonists’ opposition to the recent parliamentary acts has become indefensible, as their protests are threatening the happiness of the colonies.
The colonists should not protest against recent acts of Parliament, as these acts are in keeping with principles of justice and freedom.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article printed in the *Boston News-Letter* in 1773. That same year, the British Parliament passed the Tea Act, giving the British East India Company sole control over tea sales in the American colonies.**

The inhabitants of this town, ever attentive to the rights and interest of their country, having been repeatedly alarmed with the measures of [[☃ definition 1]] years adopted and pursued by the British administration under various forms, evidently [[☃ definition 2]] to every principle of our constitution, and after flattering ourselves from time to time with hopes that, from a change of men or some other happy circumstance, such new measures might be adopted as would put an end to the unhappy contest between Britain and the colonies and leave us in the full enjoyment of the rights which no power on earth can reasonably dispute, much less pretend to deprive us of, have yet the misfortune to find the British ministry so far from relaxing that they are still pursuing with [[☃ definition 3]] the same destructive measures . . .  do think it our duty on this, as on several other similar occasions, to express our firm resolution not only to oppose this step as dangerous to the liberty and commerce of this country, but also to aid and support our [[☃ definition 4]] in their opposition to this, and every other violation of our rights . . . 

**Which statement best describes the author’s purpose for writing this excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: people who are part of one’s group or society

choices.content: To discourage violent forms of protest
To encourage colonial opposition to British laws
To promote the benefits of the new British laws
To inspire colonists to write a new constitution

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from “An Old Prophet,” an article printed in the *New York-Gazetteer* in 1773. That same year, the British Parliament passed the Tea Act, giving the British East India Company sole control over tea sales in the American colonies.**

**QUERY.** What will be the most [[☃ definition 1]] methods of proceeding to obtain a repeal of the said oppressive, unconstitutional act?

**ANSWER.** TO USE NO TEA, *at least for the present,* for if any person should give to the sellers more than the usual price for tea, he ought to be held up as a mortal enemy to *American freedom.* And—*brave Americans,*

ASSOCIATE WITHOUT [[☃ definition 2]].   
O ye *brave men,* in whom we still may find,   
A love of virtue, FREEDOM, and mankind,   
Go forth—in Majesty of WOE [[☃ definition 5]],   
See, at your feet your COUNTRY kneels for aid,  
Be, as *one man*—[[☃ definition 3]] success [[☃ definition 4]]—  
There’s not an HONEST HEART but is yours,  
Go forth you must—ye cannot be withstood—  
Be your hearts honest, as your CAUSE *is* GOOD.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s advice?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: being divided or distinct from one another

choices.content: Americans should take up arms and revolt against the British government.
Americans should follow the law and pay higher prices for tea.
Americans should ask their representatives to repeal the Tea Act.
Americans should boycott tea until Parliament cancels the Tea Act.
